skill.name: web-browser
skill.description: Controls a local Chrome or Chromium browser through the Chrome DevTools Protocol to navigate pages, inspect elements, run JavaScript, and take screenshots.
skill.summary: Browse and inspect live web pages through a local Chrome DevTools session.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many alerts; lockfile C2, weak-crypto, markdown backtick, and several hardcoded URL alerts are false positives. Real risks remain because the skill can copy a Chrome profile with cookies and logins, expose a localhost Chrome DevTools endpoint, execute arbitrary JavaScript in authenticated pages, and print selected page content. I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration, so this is high risk but not a critical block.

high_findings.title: Browser Profile Copy Can Expose Credentials
high_findings.description: The --profile mode copies the default Chrome profile, including cookies and logins, into a cache directory and launches it with remote debugging. This is dangerous because any local process that can reach the debugging endpoint may interact with authenticated sessions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly documents and implements copying the Chrome profile with cookies and logins before enabling remote debugging. The behavior is intentional and materially exposes browser session data.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution in Active Tab
high_findings.description: The eval helper builds an AsyncFunction from command-line input and executes it in the current page. This is an intended browser-control feature, but it can read page data, mutate pages, submit forms, or expose sensitive authenticated content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs and executes JavaScript from user-provided command-line input inside the active browser page. The semantic context confirms the feature is powerful and high impact.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Execution with Environment-Derived Paths
medium_findings.description: The startup script runs shell commands to kill Chrome, create a cache directory, copy profile data, and spawn Chrome. The rsync command interpolates HOME into a shell string, so a hostile environment can increase command-execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external commands are real and mostly hardcoded, but HOME is inserted into an execSync shell string. This is a credible local risk, although not evidence of remote exploitation by itself.

medium_findings.title: Local Chrome DevTools Endpoint Controls Browser State
medium_findings.description: Multiple helpers connect to http://localhost:9222 and control the active browser. This local network endpoint is expected for the skill, but it can control tabs and authenticated pages if another local process can access it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same localhost CDP endpoint is used throughout the scripts and is the core automation mechanism. The risk is clear, but the endpoint is local and part of the intended design.

medium_findings.title: Element Picker Can Output Sensitive Page Content
medium_findings.description: The element picker returns selected element text, class names, IDs, parent context, and a slice of outer HTML. This can disclose sensitive page content when used on authenticated or private pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly gathers and prints DOM text and HTML for selected elements. The operation is user initiated, so the risk is disclosure through expected output rather than hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged command snippets in SKILL.md as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines are markdown code fences and usage examples, not executable code inside the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is visibly inside markdown code blocks. It documents commands users may run, but the markdown itself does not execute them.

low_findings.title: Lockfile Keyword and Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword, weak cryptography, and archive-library alerts in pnpm-lock.yaml are dependency names, metadata, or integrity hashes. I did not find evidence that these lines implement command-and-control logic or cryptographic behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are package entries, dependency references, or SHA512 integrity strings in a lockfile. There is no active code path or malicious intent in the cited lockfile content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Browser Automation Capability Combination
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines local browser control, optional profile copying, JavaScript execution, filesystem writes, and external commands. This combination is powerful enough to expose authenticated browser data even without confirmed malicious intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static heuristic is supported by semantic context across the scripts. The combination is intentional browser automation, but the credential and session exposure risk is real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Profile Copy Can Expose Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The --profile mode copies the default Chrome profile, including cookies and logins, into a cache directory and launches it with remote debugging. This is dangerous because any local process that can reach the debugging endpoint may interact with authenticated sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script explicitly documents and implements copying the Chrome profile with cookies and logins before enabling remote debugging. The behavior is intentional and materially exposes browser session data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution in Active Tab
dangerous_patterns.description: The eval helper builds an AsyncFunction from command-line input and executes it in the current page. This is an intended browser-control feature, but it can read page data, mutate pages, submit forms, or expose sensitive authenticated content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly constructs and executes JavaScript from user-provided command-line input inside the active browser page. The semantic context confirms the feature is powerful and high impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution with Environment-Derived Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The startup script runs shell commands to kill Chrome, create a cache directory, copy profile data, and spawn Chrome. The rsync command interpolates HOME into a shell string, so a hostile environment can increase command-execution risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external commands are real and mostly hardcoded, but HOME is inserted into an execSync shell string. This is a credible local risk, although not evidence of remote exploitation by itself.

content.user_title: Control Web Pages from Claude or Codex
content.value_statement: Manual web inspection is slow when an agent needs live page context. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code controlled browser navigation, screenshots, JavaScript evaluation, and element picking.
actual_capabilities: Starts a local Chrome browser with a fresh profile or a copied default profile.
Navigates the active tab or opens a new tab for a supplied URL.
Runs JavaScript expressions in the active page through Chrome DevTools Protocol.
Captures the current viewport screenshot and prints the temporary image path.
Lets a user click page elements and returns tag, ID, class, text, HTML, and parent context.
limitations: The Chrome executable path is hardcoded for macOS.
A local browser must be reachable on port 9222.
Profile mode can expose cookies, logins, and authenticated browser state.
There is no built-in domain allowlist, sandbox, or output redaction.

use_cases.title: Inspect live pages during research
use_cases.description: Navigate public pages, capture screenshots, and extract visible page details for a research task.

use_cases.title: Debug front-end behavior
use_cases.description: Run targeted JavaScript in a live page and inspect DOM elements while investigating interface behavior.

use_cases.title: Collect UI context for agent workflows
use_cases.description: Select page elements and provide structured context for an AI agent that needs browser state.

prompt_templates.title: Open a page
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a fresh browser session, open this URL, and tell me whether the page loaded successfully: [URL].

prompt_templates.title: Capture evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to [URL], take a screenshot, and summarize the visible page state without entering private information.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect an element
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the element picker to inspect the main call-to-action on [URL]. Report its visible text and nearby context.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate page state
prompt_templates.prompt: Open [URL], run a safe read-only JavaScript check for the page title and link count, then explain what you found.

output_examples.input: Check whether a documentation page loaded and capture the current view.
output_examples.output: The page loaded successfully. A screenshot was saved to a temporary file, and the visible content shows the documentation header and navigation.

output_examples.input: Pick the submit button on a form page.
output_examples.output: The selected element is a button with visible submit text. It appears inside the main form area and can be referenced for follow-up actions.

output_examples.input: Read the title and count links on a public page.
output_examples.output: The page title was returned, and the link count indicates the page has many navigation and content links.

best_practices: Use a fresh browser profile unless an authenticated session is strictly required.
Run read-only JavaScript checks when inspecting pages you do not control.
Close the Chrome DevTools session after use to reduce local exposure.
anti_patterns: Do not use profile mode on shared or untrusted machines.
Do not run JavaScript that changes account settings, payments, or private data.
Do not paste secrets or private page content into prompts unless the user explicitly approves.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web by itself?
faq.answer: It controls a local Chrome browser through Chrome DevTools Protocol. It still depends on the agent and user instructions.

faq.question: Can it use my logged-in browser session?
faq.answer: Yes, profile mode can copy your Chrome profile. This is high risk because cookies and logins may be exposed.

faq.question: Does it work on every operating system?
faq.answer: No. The startup script uses a macOS Chrome path, so other systems need changes.

faq.question: Can it execute JavaScript on pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can run JavaScript in the active tab, so commands should be reviewed carefully before use.

faq.question: Where are screenshots saved?
faq.answer: Screenshots are saved in the system temporary directory, and the script prints the file path.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive websites?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can expose authenticated page content, browser state, and selected DOM data.
